questions.q: Why is storing passwords in plaintext dangerous?
options: It makes password entry slower
It allows anyone with database access to immediately use the password; if the database is breached, all user accounts are compromised
It prevents users from changing their passwords
It requires more storage space
questions.explanation: Plaintext passwords offer zero protection against database breaches. An attacker with database access immediately gains all passwords and can log in as users or reuse passwords on other services (credential stuffing). Passwords must be hashed using slow, salted algorithms (bcrypt, Argon2). Even salted hashes can be cracked if the algorithm is weak, but proper hashing significantly increases time and cost.

questions.q: What is the primary benefit of Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA)?
options: It makes passwords longer and more complex
It requires multiple forms of verification, so compromising one factor does not grant full access
It encrypts all user passwords
It prevents users from forgetting their passwords
questions.explanation: MFA requires two or more factors from different categories: something you know (password), something you have (TOTP app, hardware key), or something you are (biometric). If an attacker steals the password, they still need the second factor. MFA reduces account compromise risk by ~99%. Common factors: TOTP (Google Authenticator), push notifications, hardware keys (YubiKey), SMS (less secure). SMS should not be used alone due to SIM swapping attacks.

questions.q: How can an attacker exploit weak session management?
options: By guessing the computer's model number
By stealing or predicting session tokens to impersonate authenticated users without knowing their password
By modifying system date and time
By changing the network SSID
questions.explanation: Session tokens (cookies, JWTs) are used to maintain authenticated state. Weak session management includes predictable tokens, no expiration, no HTTPS transmission, or lack of CSRF protection. An attacker can steal tokens via XSS or packet sniffing, or brute-force weak tokens. Prevention: use cryptographically random, long tokens; set short expiration; use secure, httpOnly cookies; implement CSRF tokens; and use HTTPS exclusively.

options: Physically stealing someone's business card
Using leaked passwords from one breach to attempt login on other services, leveraging password reuse
Encrypting passwords in a credential manager
Generating random passwords for security
questions.explanation: Credential stuffing exploits the common practice of password reuse across services. Attackers obtain a leaked username-password list from one breach (or dark web), then try those credentials on other services (email, banking, social media). Many accounts succeed because users reuse passwords. Prevention: require unique passwords (use password managers), implement rate limiting on login attempts, detect unusual login locations, and monitor for compromised credentials.

questions.q: How does a brute-force attack work against password authentication?
options: By tricking users into revealing their password through social engineering
By systematically trying all possible password combinations until one succeeds, feasible if no rate limiting or account lockout is in place
By crashing the server to cause a denial of service
By intercepting the password during transmission
questions.explanation: Brute-force attacks try every possible password (or common passwords) until one works. Without defenses, an 8-character password can be cracked in hours. Prevention: implement exponential backoff (increase delay between attempts), account lockout after N failures, CAPTCHA challenges, rate limiting per IP, and MFA to make brute-force useless. Encourage long, complex passwords and unique passwords per service.

questions.q: What role does salting play in password security?
options: It increases the password's length automatically
It adds a random value to the password before hashing, ensuring identical passwords hash differently and preventing rainbow table attacks
It encrypts the password so the server cannot read it
It allows users to change passwords more frequently
questions.explanation: A salt is a random value combined with the password before hashing. Without salt, identical passwords hash identically, enabling rainbow table attacks (precomputed hash lookups). With salt, the same password produces different hashes for each user. Salts are stored plaintext in the database—their security comes from uniqueness, not secrecy. Proper salting is crucial for password databases; use bcrypt or Argon2 which handle salting automatically.

questions.q: What is the weakness of password-only authentication?
options: Passwords are too easy to remember
Passwords can be forgotten, stolen, reused across services, or brute-forced; single-factor authentication provides no recovery if the password is compromised
Passwords are encrypted when stored
Passwords require special characters
questions.explanation: Password-only (single-factor) authentication is vulnerable to theft (phishing, keyloggers, MITM), reuse (credential stuffing), and weak passwords (brute-force). Once compromised, there's no second barrier. MFA mitigates this: even if the password is stolen, the attacker needs the second factor. Passwordless authentication (WebAuthn, passkeys) is even stronger, eliminating phishing risk entirely.

questions.q: How can attackers bypass authentication using account enumeration?
options: By guessing the server's IP address
By exploiting differences in login response messages to determine which usernames exist, narrowing the target to valid accounts for brute-force attacks
By modifying the login form HTML
By intercepting login packets
questions.explanation: Account enumeration occurs when a login system returns different responses for invalid usernames vs. invalid passwords, or valid accounts vs. invalid ones. Example: 'User not found' vs. 'Invalid password.' Attackers use this to build a list of valid accounts, then focus brute-force efforts on those. Prevention: return identical error messages for both cases, use rate limiting regardless of username validity, and consider not confirming account existence.

questions.q: What is the security risk of storing session tokens in local storage (browser)?
options: It makes loading web pages slower
Tokens in local storage are vulnerable to XSS attacks because JavaScript can access them, allowing attackers to steal the token and hijack the session
It prevents the browser from caching web pages
It requires HTTPS to be enabled
questions.explanation: Local storage persists browser data across sessions and is accessible to JavaScript, including malicious scripts injected via XSS. Attackers steal the token and make authenticated requests as the victim. HttpOnly cookies are more secure: they're excluded from JavaScript access and sent automatically with requests, mitigating XSS token theft. Prevention: use secure, httpOnly cookies; implement CSP to prevent XSS; and validate all input.

questions.q: How does SIM swapping lead to authentication bypass?
options: By replacing the SIM card with a counterfeit
By tricking the carrier into transferring the victim's phone number to an attacker's SIM, allowing the attacker to receive SMS 2FA codes and reset passwords
By blocking SMS messages with a jammer
By modifying the phone's operating system
questions.explanation: SIM swapping is a social engineering attack where the attacker impersonates the victim to their mobile carrier, claiming they need a new SIM. The carrier transfers the phone number to the attacker's SIM. Since SMS-based 2FA and password resets rely on receiving SMS codes, the attacker receives those codes instead, gaining account access. Prevention: use strong account recovery questions, require in-person verification for SIM transfers, use TOTP or hardware keys instead of SMS, and enable carrier-level PIN protection.
